No good `six.b` is implemented as `line.encode("latin-1")`. Please either throw in this case or use `.encode("ascii")` since that is [what the spec mandates](https://dbus.freedesktop.org/doc/dbus-specification.html#auth-protocol):

> The [authentication] protocol is a line-based protocol, where each line ends with \r\n. Each line begins with an all-caps ASCII command name containing only the character range [A-Z_], a space, then any arguments for the command, then the \r\n ending the line. The protocol is case-sensitive. All bytes must be in the ASCII character set.

We force `job_type` to be "run", because tower-cli actually doesn't have a good system of defaults for fields. If we just set `default="run"` for the job_type field, then on any modify command it will _change_ the job_type to run, which is extremely undesirable. We want to interpret this kind of default as only applying when creating a new resource. This can be done with by the create command, or by the write command with --create-on-missing set.

Root tconf calls global getConfig()  and returns it as is, without copying, so any changes in downstream tconfs will affect global config. In `Mle()` constructor, `mLinkLocalAllThreadNodes` and `mRealmLocalAllThreadNodes` would be subscribed in `SetMeshLocalPrefix()` and added to the head of `mMulticastAddresses` linked-list. So when comes to`SubscribeAllRoutersMulticast()`, the list would be

> mRealmLocalAllThreadNodes->mLinkLocalAllThreadNodes-> LinkLocalAll-> RealmLocalAll-> RealmLocalAllMplForwarders -> NULL

And after `SubscribeAllRoutersMulticast()`, the list would be

>  mRealmLocalAllThreadNodes->mLinkLocalAllThreadNodes->LinkLocalAllRouters -> RealmLocalAllRouters -> LinkLocalAll -> RealmLocalAll -> RealmLocalAllMplForwarders -> NULL

So when no additional external multicast address subscription, the head of `mMulticastAddresses` list should always be `mRealmLocalAllThreadNodes`.

One useful change I might recommend here is for the polling timer to increase. It would be pretty easy for someone to fall into the trap of calling `result(timeout=None)` on an operation that will take (for example) five minutes to complete, and this method would end up polling every second -- meaning this is 300 API requests.

While there are several ways to approach this, I would recommend a recursive algorithm where the wait time is a parameter:

```python
def _poll(self, timeout=None, increment=1):
    """Poll the LRO server to see if the operation has completed. If not, sleep for
    `increment` seconds and then try again.
    """
    # Sanity check: If we have exhausted our timeout, error out.
    if timeout is not None and timeout < 0:
        raise futures.TimeoutError()

    # Poll the server. If the operation has completed, return the Operation instance
    # with the result.
    if self._get_operation().done:
        return self._last_operation

    # Okay, we are not done yet. Wait a little while and poll again.
    time.sleep(increment)
    return self._poll(
        increment=min([30, increment * 2]),
        timeout=timeout - increment,
    )
```

This will -- assuming I implemented it correctly ;-) -- poll the server at the following increments: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 30, 30, 30, 30...

One minor wording change: `could do nasty things` could be a bit more technical.  Perhaps: `will generally leave the C++ instance in an invalid state and cause undefined behavior if the C++ instance is subsequently used.`.  Undefined behavior is the C++ technical term for "could do nasty things", of course.

It doesn't automatically do nasty things: you would actually be okay if you never do anything with the object that touches the C++ instance.  You could also call the parent `__init__` *later*, as long as you do it before invoking anything needing the C++ instance.  (I don't know why you'd do this, but it's technically possible).  Also I don't think there's any reason to go into the corrupted vtable description specifically (there are many other ways this could be a problem even if the object doesn't need a vtable; also it isn't so much "corrupted" as never created in the first place).

It is difficult to explain why; simply observe that the nest can chain methods are very straightforwardly implemented given that the "nests" property is a list of lists. Previously, I did implement it as a dictionary as you suggest. The result was messy and difficult to follow code. 

To try to explain a little more, let us suppose simple sweep objects:

```
a  = ParameterSweep(p, lambda: [0, 1, 2])
b = ParameterWrapper(q)
```
The sweep object `a` does not have dependent parameters and `b` does not have independent parameters. If we were to make the `nests` property of Paramtable a dictionary as you suggest, how would this look like? what are the keys and values? It is only once we create a nest like so:

```
c = Nest([a, b])
```
That we are able to resolve that `p` is an independent parameter and `q` is a parameter which depends on `p`. It is at that point we can make a dictionary.
